questions.questionText: In adult Advanced Cardiac Life Support (ACLS), the first-line vasopressor for cardiac arrest is:
questions.optionB: Epinephrine (adrenaline) 1mg IV every 3-5 minutes — alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction increases coronary and cerebral perfusion during CPR
questions.explanation: Core concept: Epinephrine 1mg IV q3-5min is the primary vasopressor in cardiac arrest (all rhythms). Why correct: Alpha-1 vasoconstriction → increases aortic diastolic pressure → improves coronary perfusion pressure during CPR → increases chance of ROSC. Given in all rhythms: VF/pVT (after 2nd shock), PEA/asystole (as soon as IV access). Why A wrong: Vasopressin was removed as alternative from AHA 2015 guidelines. Why C wrong: Dopamine — used for post-ROSC bradycardia/hypotension, NOT during arrest. Why D wrong: Norepinephrine — post-ROSC vasopressor (septic shock), not during arrest. Clinical relevance: Amiodarone 300mg IV (then 150mg) for refractory VF/pVT. Don't give epi before first defibrillation in witnessed VF/pVT. Exam trap: Epi dose = 1mg IV (NOT weight-based in adults). Always 1mg every 3-5 minutes regardless of rhythm. In children: 0.01 mg/kg. Memory aid: 'Epi 1mg Every 3-5 min = Eternal rule in cardiac arrest.' Reference: AHA 2020 – ACLS.

questions.questionText: Shockable rhythms in cardiac arrest include:
questions.optionB: Ventricular fibrillation (VF) and pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT) — these respond to defibrillation
questions.explanation: Core concept: VF and pVT are shockable; asystole and PEA are non-shockable. Why correct: VF: chaotic, uncoordinated ventricular activity → no cardiac output. pVT: organized rapid wide-complex rhythm but no palpable pulse. Both respond to defibrillation (electrical cardioversion). Why A wrong: Asystole (flat line) and PEA (organized rhythm without pulse) are NON-SHOCKABLE → CPR + epinephrine + treat reversible causes (4H's and 4T's). Why C wrong: Bradycardia with pulse = NOT cardiac arrest. Why D wrong: AF with pulse = NOT cardiac arrest. Clinical relevance: Defibrillation: biphasic 120-200J for VF/pVT. 4H's: Hypoxia, Hypovolemia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo/Hyperkalemia. 4T's: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thromboembolism. Exam trap: Always CHECK for pulse with organized rhythm — PEA looks organized but has no pulse = non-shockable. Memory aid: 'Shock the V's (VF, VT) — Don't shock asystole.' Reference: AHA 2020 – ACLS.
questions.conceptTag: VF pVT shockable asystole PEA non-shockable

questions.questionText: The most important post-cardiac arrest intervention that improves neurological outcome is:
questions.optionB: Targeted temperature management (TTM — targeted 32-36°C for at least 24 hours — reduces cerebral metabolic demand and neuronal injury)
questions.optionC: Immediate cardiac catheterization for all patients
questions.explanation: Core concept: TTM (therapeutic hypothermia) post-cardiac arrest improves neurological outcomes. Why correct: Post-ROSC: targeted temperature 32-36°C for ≥24 hours. Reduces cerebral metabolic demand → less reperfusion injury → better neurological outcome. Initiated as early as possible after ROSC. TTM2 trial (2021): 33°C vs 36°C → no significant difference → fever prevention may be key. Why A wrong: Steroids have no proven benefit in cardiac arrest. Why C wrong: Cardiac catheterization only for STEMI/high suspicion of coronary etiology — not ALL patients. Why D wrong: Prophylactic antiarrhythmics — routine use not recommended post-arrest. Clinical relevance: Post-cardiac arrest care bundle: TTM + coronary angiography if indicated + seizure monitoring + multimodal prognostication at ≥72 hours. Exam trap: Previous guidelines recommended 32-34°C; newer evidence (TTM2) shows 33°C not superior to 36°C normothermia → key is PREVENTING FEVER. Memory aid: 'Cool the brain after cardiac arrest = better brain.' Reference: AHA 2020 – Post-Arrest Care.
